## **ACKNOWLEDGEMENT** {#acknowledgement}

We would like to take this opportunity to express our heartfelt thanks to all those who supported us in completing this project titled An AI Powered Wearable Gesture Interpretation System for Real Time Sign to Speech Translation. We are especially grateful to our project supervisor, Mr. Niraj Khadka, for his continuous guidance, helpful suggestions, and motivation throughout the project. His support greatly contributed to the successful completion of our work. We would also like to thank Prime College for providing us with the necessary environment and resources to carry out this project. Our sincere appreciation goes to the faculty members and staff of the Computer Science Department for their cooperation and support. We are thankful to our seniors and reviewers for their valuable feedback, which helped us improve our project. Lastly, we extend our gratitude to our friends and classmates for their encouragement and support during this journey.

## **ABSTRACT** {#abstract}

An AI Powered Wearable Gesture Interpretation System for Real Time Sign to Speech Translation focuses on making communication easier for individuals with speech and hearing impairments. It uses a wearable glove along with machine learning techniques to recognize hand gestures and convert them into speech in real time.The system works by using sensors such as flex sensors and an MPU6050 to capture finger movements and hand orientation. An Arduino Nano collects this data and sends it for processing, where filtering techniques and a trained machine learning model are used to identify specific gestures.Once a gesture is recognized, it is converted into text and then into speech, allowing the user to communicate more effectively. A simple interface is also provided to display the outputs and sensor data in real time.Overall, the system shows how combining artificial intelligence with wearable technology can create a practical, portable, and user-friendly solution that supports better and more inclusive communication.

**Keywords**: *Machine Learning, Flex Sensors, MPU6050, Arduino Nano, Real-Time Processing, Text-to-Speech (TTS), Assistive Technology*

## **CHAPTER 4**  {#chapter-4}

## **SYSTEM DESIGN** {#system-design}

1. ### **Design**  {#design}

System design is about planning how all parts of a project will work together. It shows how different components are connected and how data moves between them. In this project, the system is designed to build a smart glove that reads hand gestures and converts them into speech. It combines hardware like sensors and Arduino, software for processing data, and a machine learning model for prediction. All these parts work together to give fast and accurate results in real time.

![][image2]

The system architecture is carefully structured to ensure a modular, reliable, and intelligent gesture recognition process. It integrates wearable hardware, real-time signal processing, deep learning–based classification, and a speech output interface. Overall, the system is organized to allow smooth interaction between these components, enabling accurate and efficient gesture interpretation.

* **Dataset**

The dataset for this project was gathered manually using the custom-built Nexus Glove hardware. Since the system depends on physical sensor inputs such as flex sensors and an accelerometer rather than images, existing sign language datasets were not suitable.

To collect the data, a data.py script was used to establish a USB serial connection with the Arduino and capture real-time sensor readings as the user performed different gestures. For each gesture label, around 1,200 data frames were recorded at a sampling rate of approximately 100 Hz. The collected data was then saved in a file named gesture\_data.csv, organized in a structured format for further processing and model training.

Fields in gesture\_data.csv:

* Flex1 – Flex5: These represent the analog values from the five flex sensors placed on each finger. The readings range from 0 to 1023, depending on how much each finger is bent.  
* AccX, AccY, AccZ: These are the raw accelerometer readings obtained from the MPU6050 sensor along the X, Y, and Z axes, capturing the hand’s orientation and movement.  
* Label: This indicates the gesture name (such as *“hello”*, *“water”*, *“yes”*) associated with the recorded sensor data.

This well-structured dataset forms the core input for training the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model. To enhance performance and ensure better generalization, preprocessing steps were applied. Minority classes were balanced through oversampling to match the size of the largest class. Additionally, Gaussian noise (with a mean of 0 and standard deviation of 5\) was added to each sample, generating one extra synthetic instance per frame and effectively doubling the dataset size.

As a result, the final augmented dataset used for evaluation consists of 4,704 test samples, achieving an overall test accuracy of 87%.

* ## **Intelligence Layer Signal Processing and Deep Learning (Python)**

After the sensor data reaches the laptop, it is processed through a structured six-step pipeline built in Python.

**i. Serial Reader (pyserial):**  
 The system uses the pyserial library to continuously read incoming data from the Arduino via the serial port. Each line of data is received in CSV format and converted into eight numerical values.

**ii. Accelerometer Calibration:**  
 At the beginning, the system collects around 30 frames while the hand remains still. It calculates the average accelerometer values and stores them as a baseline. This baseline is then subtracted from future readings so that the system focuses on relative motion rather than absolute position, improving consistency across users.

**iii. EMA Signal Smoothing:**  
 Sensor data often contains noise and small fluctuations. To stabilize this, an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) filter is applied. This method gives more importance to recent values while still considering past data, resulting in smoother and more reliable signals.

**iv. Feature Engineering:**  
 The processed sensor data is expanded into a richer feature set. In addition to the original values, new features like hand tilt (pitch and roll), motion intensity, and flex-based metrics are calculated. These extra features help the model better distinguish between similar gestures.

**v. Normalization (StandardScaler):**  
 Since sensor values vary in scale (flex sensors vs accelerometer), normalization is applied using Z-score scaling. This ensures all features are on a similar scale, allowing the model to treat them equally during learning.

**vi. MLP Neural Network:**  
 The final feature vector is passed into a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model. The architecture was optimized using GridSearch and includes three hidden layers (128, 64, and 32 neurons). The output layer uses Softmax to assign probabilities to each gesture class, enabling accurate classification.

## **Confidence Gate Reliability Filtering**

After prediction, the system checks how confident the model is. If the confidence is 90% or higher, the gesture is accepted. Otherwise, it is ignored and shown as *“Searching…”*. This avoids incorrect outputs, especially during transitions between gestures.

## Output Layer Speech and Dashboard

Once a gesture is confirmed, the system provides output in two ways:

**i. Speech Output:**  
 The recognized gesture is converted into speech using the system’s built-in voice engine. A control mechanism ensures speech is only triggered when the system is active, and a delay prevents repeated outputs for the same gesture.

**ii. Web Dashboard:**  
 A Flask API serves real-time data to a web interface. It provides endpoints for prediction, recalibration, retraining, and system health. The frontend displays gesture results, confidence levels, and sensor values live on the dashboard.

## **Final System Output**

The entire process from gesture input to output—takes less than 50 milliseconds. The system continuously runs in real-time, allowing smooth, fast, and natural interaction between the user and the glove.

1. #### **Refinement of Class Diagram** {#refinement-of-class-diagram}

The refined class diagram is used to present a detailed view of all the classes in the system and the relationships between them. It helps in understanding how different components interact to perform the overall functionality of the system.

![][image3]

Figure 4.2 shows the refined class diagram of the proposed system. This diagram expands the overall structure by including key classes responsible for data collection, processing, machine learning, and real-time gesture interpretation.

The DataCollector class is responsible for collecting gesture data from the wearable device. It reads sensor values from the Arduino through serial communication, performs calibration, and records labeled gesture data. This data is then used for training the model.

The Trainer class manages the machine learning process. It loads and cleans the collected dataset, performs data balancing and augmentation, trains the model using the GesturePipeline, and evaluates its performance. The trained model is then saved for real-time use.

The GesturePipeline class represents the core machine learning pipeline. It includes data preprocessing using a scaler and classification using a neural network model. The LabelEncoder class works alongside it to convert gesture labels into numerical format and back into human readable form.

The EMAFilter class is used for smoothing the incoming sensor signals. It reduces noise using an exponential moving average method, ensuring stable and accurate predictions. The FeatureExtractor class converts raw sensor data into meaningful features such as hand orientation, motion magnitude, and finger statistics.

The NexusGlovePredictor class acts as the main controller of the system. It connects to the wearable device, performs calibration, processes real-time data, and predicts gestures using the trained model. It also converts the predicted gestures into speech output, enabling sign-to-speech translation.

The FlaskAPI class provides communication between the backend and the frontend interface. It continuously processes incoming data and sends prediction results through API endpoints, allowing the web dashboard to display real-time gesture information.

Overall, this refined class diagram provides a complete view of the system, showing how raw sensor data from the wearable device is transformed into meaningful speech output. It clearly illustrates the collaboration between data collection, machine learning, and real-time processing components.

2. #### **Refinement of Sequence Diagram** {#refinement-of-sequence-diagram}

A refined sequence diagram illustrates the interactions between different components in a system over time. It provides a clear and structured view of how data flows and how different modules communicate to achieve a specific task.
